content:
# Exodus Chapter 39 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 110–112; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 82–83 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_039_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_039_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_039_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_039_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_039_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Woven Garments for Ministry (v. 1)

**1** And from [**umin**] the blue [**hattekelet**] and the purple [**veha'argaman**] and the scarlet [**vetola'at**] yarn [**hashani**] they made [**asu**] garments of [**bigdey**] service [**serad**] for ministering [**lesharet**] in the holy place [**baqqodesh**]; and they made [**vayya'asu**] the holy [**haqqodesh**] garments [**et-bigdey**] that [**asher**] were for Aaron [**le'Aharon**], as [**ka'asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] commanded [**tsivvah**] Moses [**et-Mosheh**].

---

## The Ephod (vv. 2–7)

**2** And he made [**vayya'as**] the ephod [**et-ha'efod**] of gold [**zahav**], blue [**tekelet**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet [**vetola'at shani**] yarn and fine twisted [**veshesh moshzar**] linen.

**3** And they hammered out [**vayeratsequ**] the plates of [**et-pachey**] the gold [**hazzahav**] and he cut [**veqitssets**] threads [**petilim**] to work [**la'asot**] in the midst of [**betokh**] the blue [**hattekelet**] and in the midst of [**uvetokh**] the purple [**ha'argaman**] and in the midst of [**uvetokh**] the scarlet [**tola'at**] yarn [**hashani**] and in the midst of [**uvetokh**] the fine linen [**hashesh**] — the work of [**ma'aseh**] a skilled craftsman [**choshev**].

**4** Shoulder pieces [**khetfot**] they made [**asu**] for it [**lo**], joined together [**choverot**]; at [**al**] its two [**shney**] ends [**qetsotav**] it was joined [**chubbar**].

**5** And the skillfully woven band of [**vechesheet**] his ephod [**afuddato**] that [**asher**] was on it [**alav**] — from it [**mimmennu**] it was [**hu**], according to its workmanship [**kema'asehu**] — of gold [**zahav**], blue [**tekelet**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet [**vetola'at shani**] yarn and fine twisted [**veshesh moshzar**] linen, as [**ka'asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] commanded [**tsivvah**] Moses [**et-Mosheh**].

**6** And they made [**vayya'asu**] the onyx [**avney**] stones [**hashoham**] enclosed [**musabbbot**] in settings [**mishbetsot**] of gold [**zahav**], engraved [**mefuttachot**] with the engravings of [**pittuchey**] a signet [**chotam**] according to [**al**] the names of [**shemot**] the sons of [**beney**] Israel [**Yisra'el**].

**7** And he set [**vayyasem**] them [**otam**] on [**al**] the shoulder pieces of [**kitfot**] the ephod [**ha'efod**] — stones of [**avney**] memorial [**zikkaron**] for the sons of [**livney**] Israel [**Yisra'el**] — as [**ka'asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] commanded [**tsivvah**] Moses [**et-Mosheh**].

---

## The Breastpiece (vv. 8–21)

**8** And he made [**vayya'as**] the breastpiece [**et-hachoshen**], the work of [**ma'aseh**] a skilled craftsman [**choshev**], like the work of [**kema'aseh**] the ephod [**efod**] — of gold [**zahav**], blue [**tekelet**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet [**vetola'at shani**] yarn and fine twisted [**veshesh moshzar**] linen.

**9** It was square [**ravua**]; they made [**asu**] the breastpiece [**et-hachoshen**] doubled over [**kaful**] — a span [**zeret**] its length [**orkho**] and a span [**vezeret**] its width [**rochbo**], doubled over [**kaful**].

**10** And they set [**vayemal'u**] in it [**vo**] four [**arba'ah**] rows [**turey**] of stone [**even**]: a row [**tur**] of ruby [**odem**], topaz [**pitdah**], and emerald [**uvareqet**] — the first [**ha'echad**] row [**hattur**].

**11** And the second [**hasheni**] row [**vehattur**]: turquoise [**nofekh**], sapphire [**sappir**], and diamond [**veyahalom**].

**12** And the third [**hashlishi**] row [**vehattur**]: jacinth [**leshem**], agate [**shevo**], and amethyst [**ve'achlamah**].

**13** And the fourth [**harevi'i**] row [**vehattur**]: beryl [**tarshish**], onyx [**shoham**], and jasper [**veyashfeh**] — enclosed [**musabbbot**] in settings [**mishbetsot**] of gold [**zahav**] in their mountings [**bemillu'otam**].

**14** And the stones [**veha'avanim**] were according to [**al**] the names of [**shemot**] the sons of [**beney**] Israel [**Yisra'el**] — they [**hennah**] were twelve [**shtem esreh**], according to [**al**] their names [**shemotam**], the engravings of [**pittuchey**] a signet [**chotam**] — each one [**ish**] according to [**al**] his name [**shemo**] for the twelve [**lishtey esreh**] tribes [**shavet**].

**15** And they made [**vayya'asu**] on [**al**] the breastpiece [**hachoshen**] chains [**sharshot**] of twisted work [**gablut**], the work of [**ma'aseh**] braided cord [**avot**], of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**].

**16** And they made [**vayya'asu**] two [**shtey**] settings [**mishbetsot**] of gold [**zahav**] and two [**ushney**] rings [**tabe'ot**] of gold [**zahav**], and they put [**vayyittenu**] the two [**shney**] rings [**hattabe'ot**] on [**al**] the two [**shney**] ends of [**qetsot**] the breastpiece [**hachoshen**].

**17** And they put [**vayyittenu**] the two [**shtey**] braided cords [**ha'avotot**] of gold [**hazzahav**] on [**al**] the two [**shney**] rings [**hattabe'ot**] on [**al**] the ends of [**qetsot**] the breastpiece [**hachoshen**].

**18** And the two [**shtey**] ends of [**qetsot**] the two [**shtey**] braided cords [**ha'avotot**] they put [**natenu**] on [**al**] the two [**shtey**] settings [**hammishbetsot**], and they put them [**vayyittenu**] on [**al**] the shoulder pieces of [**kitfot**] the ephod [**ha'efod**] on the front of it [**el-mul panav**].

**19** And they made [**vayya'asu**] two [**shtey**] rings [**tabe'ot**] of gold [**zahav**] and set them [**vayyasimu**] on [**al**] the two [**shney**] ends of [**qetsot**] the breastpiece [**hachoshen**], on [**al**] its edge [**sefato**] that [**asher**] was on the inner side of [**el-ever**] the ephod [**ha'efod**] inward [**baytah**].

**20** And they made [**vayya'asu**] two [**shtey**] rings [**tabe'ot**] of gold [**zahav**] and put them [**vayyittenu**] on [**al**] the two [**shney**] shoulder pieces of [**kitfot**] the ephod [**ha'efod**] beneath [**millematah**], on the front of it [**mimul panav**], close to [**le'ummat**] its seam [**mechbarto**], above [**mimma'al**] the skillfully woven band of [**lechesheet**] the ephod [**ha'efod**].

**21** And they bound [**vayyirkesu**] the breastpiece [**et-hachoshen**] from its rings [**mittabe'otav**] to [**el**] the rings of [**tabe'ot**] the ephod [**ha'efod**] with a cord [**biftil**] of blue [**tekelet**], to be [**lihyot**] on [**al**] the skillfully woven band of [**chesheet**] the ephod [**ha'efod**], and so that the breastpiece [**hachoshen**] would not [**velo**] slip [**yizzach**] from [**me'al**] the ephod [**ha'efod**] — as [**ka'asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] commanded [**tsivvah**] Moses [**et-Mosheh**].

---

## The Robe of the Ephod (vv. 22–26)

**22** And he made [**vayya'as**] the robe of [**et-me'il**] the ephod [**ha'efod**], the work of [**ma'aseh**] a weaver [**oreg**], entirely [**kullu**] of blue [**tekelet**].

**23** And the opening of [**ufi**] the robe [**hamme'il**] was in the midst of it [**betokho**], like the opening of [**kefi**] a coat of mail [**tachra**] — a border [**safah**] around [**saviv**] its opening [**lefihu**] so that it would not [**lo**] be torn [**yiqqare'a**].

**24** And they made [**vayya'asu**] on [**al**] the hems of [**shuley**] the robe [**hamme'il**] pomegranates [**rimmoney**] of blue [**tekelet**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet [**vetola'at shani**] yarn, twisted [**moshzar**].

**25** And they made [**vayya'asu**] bells [**pa'amoney**] of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**], and they put [**vayyittenu**] the bells [**et-happa'amonim**] in the midst of [**betokh**] the pomegranates [**harimmonim**] on [**al**] the hems of [**shuley**] the robe [**hamme'il**] all around [**saviv**], in the midst of [**betokh**] the pomegranates [**harimmonim**]:

**26** a bell [**pa'amon**] and a pomegranate [**verimon**], a bell [**pa'amon**] and a pomegranate [**verimon**], on [**al**] the hems of [**shuley**] the robe [**hamme'il**] all around [**saviv**], for ministering [**lesharet**] — as [**ka'asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] commanded [**tsivvah**] Moses [**et-Mosheh**].

---

## The Tunics, Turban, Sashes, and Undergarments (vv. 27–29)

**27** And they made [**vayya'asu**] the tunics [**et-hakkottonot**] of fine linen [**shesh**], the work of [**ma'aseh**] a weaver [**oreg**], for Aaron [**le'Aharon**] and for his sons [**ulevanav**].

**28** And [**ve'et**] the turban [**hammitsnefet**] of fine linen [**shesh**], and the ornamental [**pe'arey**] caps [**hammigba'ot**] of fine linen [**shesh**], and the linen [**shesh**] undergarments [**ve'et-mikhnesey**] of fine twisted [**hameshozar**] linen [**shesh**].

**29** And the sash [**ve'et-ha'avnet**] of fine twisted [**shesh moshzar**] linen and blue [**utekelet**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet [**vetola'at shani**] yarn, the work of [**ma'aseh**] an embroiderer [**roqem**] — as [**ka'asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] commanded [**tsivvah**] Moses [**et-Mosheh**].

---

## The Holy Crown (vv. 30–31)

**30** And they made [**vayya'asu**] the plate of [**et-tsits**] the holy [**haqqodesh**] crown [**nezer**] of pure [**tahor**] gold [**zahav**], and they wrote [**vayyikhtovu**] on it [**alav**] a writing [**mikhtav**] of the engravings of [**pittuchey**] a signet [**chotam**]: "Holy [**qodesh**] to YHWH [**laYHWH**]."

**31** And they put [**vayyittenu**] on it [**alav**] a cord [**petil**] of blue [**tekelet**] to set it [**latet**] on [**al**] the turban [**hammitsnefet**] above [**milma'lah**] — as [**ka'asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] commanded [**tsivvah**] Moses [**et-Mosheh**].

---

## The Completion of the Tabernacle Work (vv. 32–43)

**32** And all [**vattekhel**] the work of [**kol-avodat**] the tabernacle [**mishkan**] of the tent of [**ohel**] meeting [**mo'ed**] was completed [**vattekhel**]; and the sons of [**beney**] Israel [**Yisra'el**] did [**vayya'asu**] according to all [**kekhol**] that [**asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] commanded [**tsivvah**] Moses [**et-Mosheh**] — so [**ken**] they did [**asu**].

**33** And they brought [**vayyavi'u**] the tabernacle [**et-hammishkan**] to [**el**] Moses [**Mosheh**]: the tent [**et-ha'ohel**] and all [**ve'et-kol**] its vessels [**kelav**] — its clasps [**qerasav**], its boards [**qerashav**], its bars [**berichav**], and its pillars [**ve'ammudav**], and its bases [**va'adanav**];

**34** and the covering of [**ve'et mikhseh**] the rams' [**orot**] skins [**ha'elim**] dyed red [**hame'oddamim**], and the covering of [**ve'et mikhseh**] the tachash [**orot**] skins [**hattechashim**], and the veil of [**ve'et parokhet**] the screen [**hammasakh**];

**35** the ark of [**et-aron**] the testimony [**ha'edut**] and its poles [**ve'et-baddav**] and the mercy seat [**ve'et-hakkapporet**];

**36** the table [**et-hashulchan**], all [**et-kol**] its vessels [**kelav**] and the bread of [**ve'et lechem**] the Presence [**happanim**];

**37** the pure [**hattahor**] lampstand [**et-hammenorah**], its lamps [**et-nerotehah**] — lamps [**nerot**] of arrangement [**hamma'arakhah**] — and all [**ve'et-kol**] its vessels [**kelehah**] and the oil [**ve'et shemen**] of the light [**hamma'or**];

**38** and the golden [**hazzahav**] altar [**ve'et mizbach**] and the anointing [**hammishchah**] oil [**ve'et shemen**] and the incense [**ve'et qetoret**] of spices [**hassammim**] and the screen [**ve'et masakh**] of the entrance of [**petach**] the tent [**ha'ohel**];

**39** the bronze [**hannechoshet**] altar [**et mizbach**] and the bronze [**hannechoshet**] grating [**et-mikhbar**] that [**asher**] was for it [**lo**], its poles [**et-baddav**] and all [**ve'et-kol**] its vessels [**kelav**], the basin [**et-hakiyyor**] and its stand [**ve'et-kanno**];

**40** the hangings of [**et qal'ey**] the courtyard [**hechatser**], its pillars [**et-ammudehah**] and its bases [**ve'et-adanehah**], and the screen [**ve'et-hammasakh**] of the gate of [**lesha'ar**] the courtyard [**hechatser**], its cords [**et-metarav**] and its pegs [**viyitedotehah**], and all [**ve'et kol**] the vessels of [**keley**] the service of [**avodat**] the tabernacle [**hammishkan**] for the tent of [**le'ohel**] meeting [**mo'ed**];

**41** the garments of [**et-bigdey**] service [**hasserad**] for ministering [**lesharet**] in the holy place [**baqqodesh**], the holy [**haqqodesh**] garments [**et-bigdey**] for Aaron [**le'Aharon**] the priest [**hakkohen**] and the garments of [**ve'et-bigdey**] his sons [**banav**] for serving as priests [**lekhahhen**].

**42** According to all [**kekhol**] that [**asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] commanded [**tsivvah**] Moses [**et-Mosheh**], so [**ken**] the sons of [**beney**] Israel [**Yisra'el**] did [**asu**] all [**et kol**] the work [**ha'avodah**].

**43** And Moses [**Mosheh**] saw [**vayyar**] all [**et-kol**] the work [**hammelakhah**], and behold [**vehinneh**], they had done [**asu**] it [**otah**] — as [**ka'asher**] YHWH [**YHWH**] had commanded [**tsivvah**], so [**ken**] they had done [**asu**]; and Moses [**Mosheh**] blessed them [**vayevarekh otam**].

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

1. **Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 110–112:** The text of Exodus 39 begins on folio 110 (page 1 image), picking up from the materials accounting of chapter 38. The three-column layout continues throughout all three folios. The chapter opens with the fabrication of the priestly service garments from the blue, purple, and scarlet yarns first collected in 35:25. Across folio 110, the ephod construction (vv. 2–7) and the breastpiece (vv. 8–21) occupy the majority of the text. The Tiberian vowel pointing and accentuation are fully legible, including the careful vocalization of the gemstone names in vv. 10–13, several of which are hapax or near-hapax forms (e.g., leshem, shevo, achlamah, tarshish, yashfeh). On folio 111 (page 2 image), the chapter continues with the robe of the ephod (vv. 22–26), the subsidiary garments (vv. 27–29), and the golden plate (vv. 30–31), followed by the completion and inspection pericope (vv. 32–43). The refrain "ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Mosheh" (as YHWH commanded Moses) recurs seven times in this chapter (vv. 1, 5, 7, 21, 26, 29, 31) and then an additional two occurrences in the inspection section (vv. 32, 42–43), forming a sustained obedience formula that punctuates the entire garment narrative. Folio 112 (page 3 image) shows the chapter's conclusion, with the detailed inventory list of vv. 33–41 and the final blessing of v. 43. A parashah setumah division appears before v. 32, marking the transition from individual garment descriptions to the comprehensive completion formula. The Masoretic notation is consistent throughout, and plene/defective spellings follow standard Leningrad Codex practice.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 82–83:** The Vaticanus Greek text on pages 82–83 presents the LXX version of the priestly garment construction. The well-known divergence between LXX and MT ordering persists: the LXX groups the construction material somewhat differently from the MT, and the chapter/verse numbering does not precisely correspond. On page 82, the three-column uncial scriptio continua contains the Greek equivalents of the ephod (ephoud), the breastpiece (logion/logeion), and the robe (hypodutas). The gemstone names in vv. 10–13 show the expected LXX variations: sardion for odem, topazion for pitdah, smaragdos for bareqet, anthrax for nofekh, sappheiros for sappir, and so forth — a gemstone list that diverges from the MT in several identifications, reflecting the difficulty ancient translators had with mineralogical terminology. On page 83 (marked "CAP. XXXIX" at the top), the completion and inspection section is visible, with the inventory list and the final episkepsis (inspection) by Moses. The LXX rendering of v. 43 is notable: where the MT reads "vayyar Mosheh et-kol hammelakhah vehinneh asu otah" (and Moses saw all the work, and behold, they had done it), the LXX reads kai eiden Mouses panta ta erga kai esan pepoiekotes auta — a faithful rendering. The LXX also preserves the blessing formula at the chapter's end. The scriptio continua is clearly legible across all three columns on both pages, with the characteristic Vaticanus hand maintaining consistent letter forms.

### Key Translation Decisions

3. **v. 1 — "bigdey serad" (garments of service/woven garments):** The term "serad" is notoriously difficult. It appears only in the contexts of Exodus 31:10, 35:19, and 39:1, 41 — always in connection with the priestly garments used in the sanctuary. Its root is uncertain: some derive it from sarad (to remain, survive), others connect it to a weaving term. The LXX renders it with stolai leitourgikai (liturgical garments). The formal translation "garments of service" preserves the functional connotation evident in the immediate context: these garments are "for ministering in the holy place" (lesharet baqqodesh). The distinction between "bigdey serad" (service garments, v. 1a) and "bigdey haqqodesh" (holy garments, v. 1b) suggests the former may be a broader category encompassing the latter, or alternatively that "serad" garments are the textile components that underlie the holy garments proper.

4. **v. 3 — "vayeratsequ et-pachey hazzahav veqitssets petilim" (and they hammered out the plates of gold and he cut threads):** This verse describes a remarkable ancient metallurgical technique: gold was hammered into thin sheets (pachim, plates), then cut (qitssets, from the root qatsats, to cut) into thin threads (petilim) that could be woven into the fabric alongside the dyed yarns and linen. The result was a textile interwoven with actual gold wire — not merely gold-colored thread. The switch from plural "they hammered" (vayeratsequ) to singular "he cut" (veqitssets) may indicate that the gold-beating was communal work while the precision cutting of wire was the master craftsman's task. This is the only verse in the Tabernacle narrative that describes the technique of gold-thread production.

5. **vv. 6–7 — "avney shoham musabbot mishbetsot zahav" (onyx stones enclosed in settings of gold):** The two shoham stones, engraved with the names of the twelve tribes (six per stone), are mounted on the shoulder pieces of the ephod as "avney zikkaron" (stones of memorial/remembrance). The high priest literally carries Israel on his shoulders — the tribes are borne into the divine presence as engraved names on precious stone. The verb "musabbot" (enclosed, surrounded) indicates that the gold settings fully encircle each stone, securing it and framing it. The instruction parallel is 28:9–12.

6. **vv. 8–21 — The Breastpiece (choshen):** The breastpiece is the most elaborate single garment element, described in fourteen verses. Its construction mirrors the instruction of 28:15–30 with near-verbatim fidelity. It is "the work of a skilled craftsman" (ma'aseh choshev) — the same designation given to the ephod and the Tabernacle curtains, indicating the highest grade of textile art. The breastpiece is "doubled over" (kaful), forming a pouch (into which the Urim and Thummim are placed, though their placement is not mentioned here in the construction narrative). Its dimensions — a span by a span (approximately 9 inches square) — make it the smallest yet most symbolically dense element of the priestly vestments. The twelve stones arranged in four rows of three correspond to the twelve tribes, each stone engraved "pittuchey chotam" (engravings of a signet) with the tribal name. The attachment system (vv. 15–21) is described with meticulous precision: gold chains connect the breastpiece's upper corners to the ephod's shoulder pieces; gold rings and a blue cord secure the lower corners to the ephod's waistband. The purpose is explicitly stated: "so that the breastpiece would not slip from the ephod" (velo yizzach hachoshen me'al ha'efod). The high priest bears the names of Israel over his heart (28:29) — the breastpiece is the complement to the shoulder stones, together ensuring that Israel is carried both on the priest's shoulders (strength) and over his heart (love).

7. **vv. 10–13 — The Twelve Gemstones:** The identification of the twelve stones remains one of the most debated questions in biblical mineralogy. The Hebrew names — odem, pitdah, bareqet, nofekh, sappir, yahalom, leshem, shevo, achlamah, tarshish, shoham, yashfeh — do not map precisely to modern gemological categories. Ancient identifications varied: the LXX translators themselves diverged, and Josephus and the Targums provide yet further alternatives. The formal translation employs conventional English renderings (ruby, topaz, emerald, turquoise, sapphire, diamond, jacinth, agate, amethyst, beryl, onyx, jasper) while acknowledging that these are traditional rather than certain identifications. What is certain is the symbolic architecture: twelve stones, twelve names, four rows of three — the fullness of Israel arranged in ordered array upon the priest's heart.

8. **vv. 22–26 — The Robe (me'il) of the Ephod:** The robe is woven entirely of blue (tekelet) — the color associated with the heavens and with divinity. Its opening has a reinforced border "like the opening of a coat of mail" (kefi tachra), preventing tearing. The hem is adorned with alternating pomegranates (of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn) and golden bells: "pa'amon verimon, pa'amon verimon" — the rhythmic alternation itself enacts the theology of the garment. The bells sound when the priest enters and exits the holy place (28:35), announcing his movements before YHWH. The pomegranates, ancient symbols of fertility and abundance, hang silent between the sounding bells. The instruction parallel is 28:31–35.

9. **v. 30 — "qodesh laYHWH" (Holy to YHWH):** The golden plate (tsits) bears the inscription "Holy to YHWH," engraved as a signet — permanent, authoritative, identifying. This plate, bound by a blue cord to the front of the turban, is the visible declaration of the priest's consecration and, through him, of Israel's consecration. The term "tsits" (from the root tsuts, to blossom, to shine) evokes both a blossom and a radiance — the plate gleams on the high priest's forehead as a flower of holiness. The instruction parallel is 28:36–38.

10. **v. 32 — "vattekhel kol-avodat mishkan ohel mo'ed" (and all the work of the tabernacle of the tent of meeting was completed):** The verb "vattekhel" (was completed, from the root k-l-h) marks the formal end of the construction narrative that began in chapter 35. The same root appears in Genesis 2:1–2, where heaven and earth are "completed" (vayekhullu) and God "completed" (vayekhal) His work — a deliberate literary echo. Just as God completed creation and rested, so Israel completes the Tabernacle and presents it for inspection. The Tabernacle is a micro-cosmos, and its completion recapitulates the completion of the macro-cosmos. The phrase "the sons of Israel did according to all that YHWH commanded Moses — so they did" (vayya'asu beney Yisra'el kekhol asher tsivvah YHWH et-Mosheh ken asu) forms a comprehensive obedience statement, with the emphatic doubling "so they did" (ken asu) providing closure.

11. **vv. 33–41 — The Inventory Presented to Moses:** The inventory list is the most comprehensive enumeration of Tabernacle components in the Torah. Every major element is presented to Moses for inspection: the tent structure (v. 33), the coverings and veil (v. 34), the ark and mercy seat (v. 35), the table and showbread (v. 36), the lampstand with its lamps, vessels, and oil (v. 37), the golden altar with anointing oil and incense (v. 38), the bronze altar with its grating and accessories (v. 39), the courtyard with all its fixtures (v. 40), and the priestly garments (v. 41). The list moves from structure to furnishings to garments — from the house itself to its contents to its servants' attire. This is not merely a checklist; it is a formal presentation, a ceremonial handing-over of completed work for authoritative inspection.

12. **v. 43 — "vayyar Mosheh et-kol hammelakhah vehinneh asu otah ka'asher tsivvah YHWH ken asu vayevarekh otam Mosheh" (And Moses saw all the work, and behold, they had done it — as YHWH had commanded, so they had done; and Moses blessed them):** This verse is the climactic conclusion of the entire construction narrative (Exodus 35–39). Its language deliberately echoes Genesis 1:31: "And God saw all that He had made, and behold, it was very good" (vayyar Elohim et-kol asher asah vehinneh tov me'od). Moses "sees" (vayyar) "all the work" (et-kol hammelakhah) and pronounces it acceptable, just as God "saw" all creation and pronounced it good. The particle "vehinneh" (and behold) marks the moment of appraisal — Moses examines and discovers conformity to the divine command. The phrase "ka'asher tsivvah YHWH ken asu" (as YHWH had commanded, so they had done) is the verdict: complete obedience, perfect correspondence between command and execution. Moses then "blessed them" (vayevarekh otam) — the content of the blessing is not specified, but the act itself seals the construction with prophetic approval. Just as God blessed the seventh day after creation's completion (Genesis 2:3), so Moses blesses the workers after the Tabernacle's completion. The Tabernacle narrative thus closes with the same structural sequence as creation: work — inspection — approval — blessing.

### Structural Notes

13. **The Sevenfold Obedience Refrain:** The phrase "ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Mosheh" (as YHWH commanded Moses) appears seven times within the garment-construction section of this chapter (vv. 1, 5, 7, 21, 26, 29, 31). This sevenfold repetition is not accidental; it mirrors the seven divine speeches of creation ("And God said," Genesis 1:3, 6, 9, 14, 20, 24, 26) and the seven acts of divine assessment ("And God saw that it was good"). The number seven — signifying completeness and sanctity — stamps the priestly garments with the mark of perfect obedience. Each garment element is sealed with the refrain: the ephod's waistband (v. 5), the shoulder stones (v. 7), the breastpiece attachment (v. 21), the robe's bells and pomegranates (v. 26), the sash (v. 29), and the golden plate (v. 31), with v. 1 providing the overarching frame. The cumulative effect is a liturgical cadence: the reader/listener encounters the refrain at each transition, building toward the comprehensive statement of v. 32.

14. **Creation-Tabernacle Parallel:** Exodus 39:32, 42–43 deliberately recapitulates Genesis 1:31–2:3. The parallels are precise: (a) "all the work was completed" (vattekhel kol-avodat, 39:32) parallels "the heavens and the earth were completed" (vayekhullu hashamayim veha'arets, Gen 2:1); (b) "Moses saw all the work" (vayyar Mosheh et-kol hammelakhah, 39:43) parallels "God saw all that He had made" (vayyar Elohim et-kol asher asah, Gen 1:31); (c) "and behold, they had done it" (vehinneh asu otah, 39:43) parallels "and behold, it was very good" (vehinneh tov me'od, Gen 1:31); (d) "and Moses blessed them" (vayevarekh otam Mosheh, 39:43) parallels "and God blessed the seventh day" (vayevarekh Elohim et-yom hashevi'i, Gen 2:3). This literary architecture establishes the Tabernacle as a new creation — a sacred space within the cosmos that recapitulates the ordering and sanctifying of the cosmos itself. If the world is God's macro-temple, the Tabernacle is Israel's micro-world, and its construction is a creation event requiring the same vocabulary of completion, inspection, approval, and blessing.

15. **The Garment-Furnishing Symmetry:** Exodus 39 mirrors Exodus 28 (the garment instructions) just as Exodus 37–38 mirrored Exodus 25–27 (the furnishing instructions). The command-fulfillment architecture of the Tabernacle narrative is now complete for both furnishings and vestments. The garments are described in the same order in both chapters: ephod (28:6–14 / 39:2–7), breastpiece (28:15–30 / 39:8–21), robe (28:31–35 / 39:22–26), tunics and sash (28:39 / 39:27–29), golden plate (28:36–38 / 39:30–31). The near-verbatim repetition between command and execution is a deliberate literary strategy: it demonstrates that Israel built exactly what God specified, neither adding nor subtracting. The formal equivalence translation preserves this repetitive correspondence, resisting any temptation to condense or vary the vocabulary between paired passages.

### Theological Notes

16. **The Priest as Living Tabernacle:** The priestly garments of Exodus 39 transform the human body into a mobile sanctuary. The ephod and breastpiece, with their gold, blue, purple, and scarlet yarns, employ the same materials as the Tabernacle curtains (26:1). The golden bells echo the golden furnishings. The blue robe mirrors the blue of the heavenly realm. The golden plate inscribed "Holy to YHWH" corresponds to the Ark's mercy seat — the most sacred element placed at the highest point. The priest, when fully vested, is a walking image of the Tabernacle: from the linen undergarments (corresponding to the innermost curtains) to the outer robe (corresponding to the tent coverings) to the ephod and breastpiece (corresponding to the sacred furnishings) to the golden crown (corresponding to the divine presence). In this way, the priest embodies the sacred space — he carries Israel's names on his shoulders and heart, bears the holiness inscription on his brow, and moves through the sanctuary as its human counterpart. The garments do not merely adorn; they consecrate. They transform a descendant of Aaron into an instrument of mediation between YHWH and Israel.

17. **"Holy to YHWH" — The Theology of Inscription:** The golden plate bearing "qodesh laYHWH" (Holy to YHWH) is the only text inscribed on the priestly garments. Its placement on the forehead — the most visible part of the human form — declares the priest's total consecration. In 28:38, this plate is said to "bear the iniquity of the holy things" (venasa Aharon et-avon haqqodashim) — the holy gifts of Israel, inevitably contaminated by human imperfection, are rendered acceptable by the declaration on the priest's brow. The inscription functions as both identity and intercession: it names the priest as belonging to YHWH and simultaneously activates a perpetual atonement for the community's sacred offerings. The engraving technique — "pittuchey chotam" (engravings of a signet) — is the same used for the tribal names on the breastpiece stones, linking the priest's identity ("Holy to YHWH") with Israel's identity (the twelve names). The priest is holy because Israel is called to be holy; Israel's offerings are acceptable because the priest bears the inscription of holiness.

18. **Moses' Blessing and the Completion of Sacred Work:** The unspecified blessing of v. 43 has generated extensive commentary. Rabbinic tradition (Sifrei, Rashi) identifies it with the words: "May it be the will [of God] that the Shekhinah rest upon the work of your hands" — a prayer that the completed structure will achieve its purpose as a dwelling for the divine presence. Whether or not this specific wording is original, the narrative logic supports the interpretation: the Tabernacle has been built precisely according to divine command; Moses has inspected and approved it; what remains is for God to inhabit it. The blessing bridges chapters 39 and 40 — from human completion to divine occupation. The workers have done their part; now they await the descent of the glory.

19. **Formal Equivalence Methodology:** This translation maintains strict word-order fidelity to the Hebrew throughout Exodus 39, preserving all waw-consecutive and waw-copulative conjunctions, retaining the repetitive garment specifications and the comprehensive inventory list without condensation or paraphrase, and providing transliterated glosses for each Hebrew term. The sevenfold obedience refrain "ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Mosheh" is rendered identically at each occurrence to preserve its liturgical cadence. The near-verbatim correspondence between this chapter and the instruction text of Exodus 28 is maintained in translation, with consistent vocabulary choices across both passages. YHWH is transliterated throughout. Standard name forms (Moses, Aaron, Israel) are used. "Sons of Israel" is preserved where the Hebrew reads "beney Yisra'el." Technical terms (efod, choshen, me'il, tsits, serad, choshev, roqem) are translated with consistent English equivalents across chapters. Gemstone names follow conventional English identifications while acknowledging their uncertainty in the notes.

--- LOGOS
